coredata.dc:title: Mitigating the interfacial concentration gradient by negatively charged quantum dots toward dendrite-free Zn anodes
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Feifei
Lu, Haotian
Zhu, Hanbing
Wang, Lu
Chen, Zihui
Yang, Chunpeng
Yang, Quan-Hong
coredata.dc:description: Aqueous zinc (Zn) batteries have shown great promise in energy storage systems as an intrinsically safe battery configuration. However, Zn dendrite and side reactions may impede their practical application. Here, we propose to mitigate interfacial concentration gradient by introducing N, S-doped carbon quantum dots (NSQDs) as an additive to achieve uniform Zn deposition. The dangling oxygen-containing groups of the NSQDs would be reduced by a Zn foil, thus interacting with the oxidized Zn2+ to form Zn-O bonds on the Zn surface. The formed NSQD layer provides abundant nucleation sites for Zn ions pre-seeding. Meantime, the NSQD layer in negative charge decreases the ion concentration gradient and uniformizes the electric field distribution near the Zn-electrolyte interfaces, facilitating dendrite-free Zn deposition, which is well evidenced by finite simulation results. Together with a decreased interfacial concentration gradient, the Zn metal anode exhibits good rate performances (20 mA cm−2 for nearly 6000 cycles). In addition, the assembled pouch full cell exhibits excellent cycling stability, and 81% of capacity retention is achieved after 250 cycles. This work not only offers a simple approach to modulate the interfacial concentration gradient for uniform Zn deposition but also paves the way for a practical dendrite-free aqueous Zn battery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zn batteries
Interfacial concentration gradient
Cycling stability
Dendrite-free

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52202279), the National Industry-Education Integration Platform of Energy Storage, Haihe Laboratory of Sustainable Chemical Transformations, and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) High concentration gradient: Zn anode with problems such as slow mass transfer, Zn dendrite formation, and HER. (b) Decreased concentration gradient: a negatively charged NSQD layer is formed to improve the Zn2+ transfer, facilitating dendrite-free deposition and HER suppression.
(a) TEM image of the NSQDs. (b) FTIR spectra of the NSQDs. Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) N1s, (d) S2p, and (e) C1s. (f) Model of the prepared NSQDs.

. (a) Zeta potential of NSQDs in neutral solution before and after ZnSO4 addition. (b) SEM and corresponding mapping of the Zn foil soaked with the NSQD dispersion. Deposition diagram and SEM images of Zn deposition on Zn electrode at 5 mA cm−2. (c) 0.5, (d) 1 and (e) 2 mAh cm−2 for NSQDs-containing electrolyte; (f) 0.5, (g) 1 and (h) 2 mAh cm−2 for the normal electrolyte. Numerical simulation of the potential distribution in (i) normal electrolytes, (j) NSQDs-containing electrolytes (NSQDs with increased zeta potential). The ion concentration distribution in (k) normal electrolytes, (l) NSQDs-containing electrolytes (NSQDs with increased zeta potential).

. (a) EIS spectra of the Zn symmetric cells before/after 10 cycles with/without the NSQD additive. (b) Nucleation overpotentials test of Zn ion at 5 mA cm−2. (c) CV curves of Zn plating/stripping at 0.5 mV s−1. (d) LSV curves of Zn anode with/without the NSQD additive. (e) Linear polarization curves showing the corrosion rate of Zn foil with/without the NSQD additive. (f) XRD patterns of Zn anode soaked in 2 M ZnSO4 with/without NSQDs.

Performance of the batteries with/without the NSQD additive. (a) Rate performance of Zn symmetric cells during Zn stripping/plating at current densities from 0.2 to 20 mA cm−2. (b) Cycling performance of Zn symmetric cells at 2 mA cm−2 with the plating/stripping capacity of 0.5 mAh cm−2. (c) Cycling performance of Zn symmetric cells at 20 mA cm−2 (1 mAh cm−2). SEM images after 10 cycles at 10 mA cm−2 (0.5 mAh cm−2) without NSQDs additive (d) and with the NSQD additive (e). (f) CE curves of Zn//Cu batteries with/without the NSQD additive.

Full battery performance with NSQDs. (a) Voltage profiles of Zn//ZVO with NSQDs at various charge/discharge current densities. (b) Comparison of the rate performance of Zn//ZVO with/without NSQDs. (c) Capacity retention and CE curves of the Zn//ZVO battery in a cycling test at 4 A g−1. (d) Cycling stability of the full cell with NSQDs with the ZVO loading of 4.4 mg cm−2. (e) Cycling stability of the pouch cell with NSQDs.

head.ce:title: Mitigating the interfacial concentration gradient by negatively charged quantum dots toward dendrite-free Zn anodes

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aqueous zinc (Zn) batteries have shown great promise in energy storage systems as an intrinsically safe battery configuration. However, Zn dendrite and side reactions may impede their practical application. Here, we propose to mitigate interfacial concentration gradient by introducing N, S-doped carbon quantum dots (NSQDs) as an additive to achieve uniform Zn deposition. The dangling oxygen-containing groups of the NSQDs would be reduced by a Zn foil, thus interacting with the oxidized Zn2+ to form Zn-O bonds on the Zn surface. The formed NSQD layer provides abundant nucleation sites for Zn ions pre-seeding. Meantime, the NSQD layer in negative charge decreases the ion concentration gradient and uniformizes the electric field distribution near the Zn-electrolyte interfaces, facilitating dendrite-free Zn deposition, which is well evidenced by finite simulation results. Together with a decreased interfacial concentration gradient, the Zn metal anode exhibits good rate performances (20 mA cm−2 for nearly 6000 cycles). In addition, the assembled pouch full cell exhibits excellent cycling stability, and 81% of capacity retention is achieved after 250 cycles. This work not only offers a simple approach to modulate the interfacial concentration gradient for uniform Zn deposition but also paves the way for a practical dendrite-free aqueous Zn battery.

Since the interfacial concentration gradient has a direct impact on Zn deposition, we propose to use the N, S-doped quantum dots (NSQDs) layer in negative charge to decrease the ion concentration gradient and uniformize the electric field distribution near the Zn-electrolyte interfaces, facilitating the dendrite-free Zn deposition. As a result, the Zn symmetrical cell with the NSQDs additives exhibits a long lifespan of nearly 6000 cycles at 20 mA cm−2.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous zinc (Zn) batteries have aroused wide research interest due to their high theoretical volumetric capacity (5855 mAh cm−3), low redox potential (−0.762 V vs. standard hydrogen electrode, SHE), and intrinsic safety [1–4]. However, the dendrite growth and parasitic reactions at the Zn-electrolyte interface such as hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and corrosion have hindered their wide application (Scheme 1 a) [5,6]. Due to the low Zn/Zn2+ redox potential, HER inevitably occurs on the Zn-electrolyte interface during Zn deposition, resulting in the decreased Coulombic efficiency (CE) and cycling stability of the Zn batteries [7–9]. Moreover, the dendrite growth due to a “tip effect” aggravates side reactions and possible separator penetration, resulting in a short circuit during the charge/discharge [10]. It is, therefore, necessary to develop stable Zn anodes with uniform Zn deposition/dissolution and reduced side reactions to endow the Zn batteries with a long lifespan.

To alleviate the dendrite issues, various strategies have been adopted to improve the cycling stability of the Zn anode, such as interfacial modification [11–20], electrolyte optimization [21–24], and a three-dimensional scaffold [25–28]. Electrolyte additives have been widely recognized as an effective method to induce dendrite-free Zn deposition by the electrostatic shielding effect or modulation of the Zn2+ solvation structures [29–35]. However, few of these works focus on the effect of the interfacial concentration gradient on the Zn deposition process, especially at a high rate. In particular, a high interfacial concentration gradient not only decreases the reaction kinetics but also accelerates the dendrite growth due to preferential Zn2+ aggregation on the “tips”. Therefore, the rational design of electrolyte additives to mitigate the interfacial concentration gradient shows great potential in suppressing dendrite growth and parasitic side reactions.

Here, we propose to homogenize the Zn nucleation and deposition by mitigating the interfacial concentration gradient by adding N, S-doped carbon quantum dots (NSQDs) into the electrolyte. Due to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, the NSQDs react with the Zn foil, producing the charged NSQD layer. This formed NSQD layer provides abundant nucleation sites for the Zn ions pre-seeding and nucleation. Meanwhile, the negatively charged carbon dots near the Zn surface decrease the ion concentration gradient and uniformize the electric field on the Zn-electrolyte interfaces, facilitating the fast Zn2+ transfer for dendrite-free Zn deposition (Scheme 1b). Following the mitigated interfacial concentration gradient, the symmetric cells with the NSQD additive exhibit a good rate performance of 20 mA cm−2 for nearly 6000 cycles. When coupled with a Zn vanadate (ZVO) cathode, the full cell shows a high capacity retention of 85.9% after cycling for 1500 cycles. Even assembled in the pouch full cell, the full cell also exhibits excellent cycling stability with 81% of capacity retention after 250 cycles. This work not only provides a renewed understanding of Zn deposition by modulating the interfacial concentration gradient but also paves the way for a practical dendrite-free aqueous Zn battery.

Using the one-step solvothermal method, we synthesized the NSQDs with citric acid as the carbon source and thiourea as the N and S sources. The transmission electron micrograph (TEM) image reveals that the as-prepared NSQDs are well dispersed in narrow size distributions of 7.23 ± 1.3 nm (Figs. 1 a and S1). The fluorescent characteristic and two prominent peaks at 1348 and 1570 cm−1 in Raman spectra are observed, manifesting the successful synthesis of the NSQDs (Fig. S2) [36,37]. The broad absorption bands at 3000–3600 cm−1 in the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra are assigned to stretching vibrations of S-H, OH and NH bonding (Fig. 1b). This indicates the ample amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface, endowing the NSQDs with good zincophilic properties. The bands at 1394 and 1715 cm−1 are respectively attributed to the vibrational absorption band of C–N and C=O. The peak at 1049 cm−1 is attributed to the C=S vibration, while the weak C–S stretching is also observed at 602.9 cm−1 [38,39]. In addition, two peaks at 399.8 and 401.7 eV are observed in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Figs. S3 and 1c), which are respectively attributed to the pyrrolic N and graphitic N or N–H bonds [40]. The high resolution of the S 2p XPS spectrum of NSQDs clearly shows three peaks at 163.1, 164.3, and 168.3 eV, which represent S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of thiophene and S=O bonding (Fig. 1d) [41]. These results indicate that S doped into the carbon dot structure and formed thiophene units. Moreover, the C=O and O-C=O can also be observed in the C1s spectra, which are consistent with the FTIR results (Fig. 1e). The NSQDs contain abundant O, N, and S-containing functional groups (Fig. 1f), which are expected to facilitate the preferential adsorption and interaction of NSQDs with the Zn anode and Zn ions [42].

The negatively charged state of the NSQDs surface is certified by the zeta potential measurements. For NSQDs dispersion, the negative value of −35.4 mV ensures the effective adsorption of Zn2+ on the NSQD surface (Fig. 2 a), implying sufficient Zn2+ supplement even in high current densities. This is also certified by the positive shift of the zeta potential after the Zn2+ adsorption, which can change the solvation structure of Zn2+, facilitating the Zn2+ transport. XPS spectra and energy-dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) mapping also certify the interaction between NSQD and Zn foil, implying the formation of the NSQD layer on the Zn surface (Figs. S4 and 2b). The negatively charged NSQD layer is expected to uniformize the electric field distribution and mitigate the Zn2+ concentration gradient near the Zn surface during plating/stripping, facilitating the fast Zn2+ transfer at a high rate and dendrite-free Zn deposition.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of Zn during the Zn deposition (Fig. 2c–e) show that the number of Zn particles increases with Zn-plating time, implying a dendrite-free Zn deposition. In contrast, increasing Zn dendrites are observed on a Zn foil with continuous Zn deposition, which is caused by random Zn deposition due to the “tip effect” (Fig. 2f–h). The NSQD layer redistributes Zn ions to achieve a uniform electric field distribution near the electrode to suppress the formation of the tips. In contrast, without the NSQD additive, the Zn ions continue to accumulate on the tips because of the inhomogeneous distribution of the electric field, which leads to the proliferation of Zn dendrites.

Numerical simulations further reveal the different electric field and concentration distributions with/without the NSQD additive, which has a direct impact on the Zn deposition behavior. Owing to the uneven surface of the Zn foil, the high potential near the protruding Zn nuclei tends to increase drastically, which causes the continuous growth of Zn dendrites (Fig. 2i). When introducing the negatively charged NSQD layer on the Zn surface, the “tip effect” is mitigated due to homogenized electric field (Fig. 2j), which increases with the higher zeta potential of the NSQD layer, facilitating the uniform Zn deposition. Moreover, in comparison to the larger Zn2+ concentration gradient for normal electrolytes (Fig. 2k), lower concentration differences are obtained when using the NSQD layer with increasing zeta potential (Fig. 2l). This decreased concentration difference not only improves the redox kinetics but also ensures the ample Zn2+ supplement at a high rate, which facilitates the dendrite-free Zn deposition. Notably, by introducing the negatively charged layer onto the Zn foil, not just NSQDs, the electric field can be homogenized while the interfacial concentration gradient can be reduced, which accelerates the Zn2+ transfer at a high rate, achieving dendrite-free Zn deposition.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements further reveal a much smaller charge transfer resistance of the battery with NSQDs, indicating fast electron transport capability (Fig. 3 a). The Zn2+ transference number (tZn 2+) also exhibits a higher value of 0.32 with the NSQD additive, which is beneficial to suppress concentration polarization and homogenize Zn2+ distribution during the Zn stripping/plating process (Fig. S5) [43]. Benefiting from the fast electron/Zn2+ transport, the reduction of Zn2+ quickly occurs at the Zn-electrolyte interface, which accelerates the plating process of Zn with low polarization and interface resistance.

Moreover, the partially reduced NSQD layer provides abundant adsorption sites, homogenizing the Zn nucleation and deposition during the plating process. The NSQDs-containing electrolyte exhibits a much lower nucleation overpotential (47.2 mV) than that of a normal electrolyte (112.6 mV) (Fig. 3b), illustrating the lower energy barrier of heterogeneous nucleation of Zn with the NSQD additive. The improved kinetics of Zn2+ was also proved by the decreased interfacial charge transfer resistance and much smaller activation energy with NSQDs additives (Fig. S6). In addition, the cyclic voltammetry curves (CV) with the NSQD additive exhibit a much stronger intensity of redox peaks (Fig. 3c), demonstrating the improved reaction kinetics, which is in line with the EIS results.

We employed Raman spectroscopy to investigate the evolution of H-bonds and the formation of solvation configurations in the electrolyte with NSQD additives (Fig. S7). Since Zn2+-H2O is regarded as the culprit of HER during the electrochemical process, the decreased content ratio of Zn2+-H2O from 3.5 to 1.9% with NSQD additives is expected to suppress HER [44]. Specifically, the limited HER is certified by a more negative HER overpotential (−1.18 V vs. SHE) in the NSQDs-containing electrolyte than that with normal electrolyte (−1.15 V vs. SHE) at 20 mA cm−2 (Fig. 3d). We also used linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) to research the Zn anticorrosion in the electrolyte with/without NSQDs. A more positive corrosion potential is obtained for the NSQDs-containing electrolyte, indicating a smaller tendency for a corrosion reaction (Fig. 3e). The soaking experiments also reveal the reduced side reactions between the Zn foil and ZnSO4 in the NSQDs-containing electrolyte, which is certified by the weaker peak intensity of Zn4SO4(OH)6•5H2O in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 3f).

Following the mitigated concentration gradient for uniform Zn deposition and fast Zn2+ transfer, the symmetric cell with NSQDs shows excellent rate performance and lower overpotentials for all current ranges (Fig. 4 a). The symmetric cell with the NSQD additive also exhibits superior cycling stability and cycling life of over 1300 h at 2 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 0.5 mAh cm−2, which is nearly 2.6 times that of normal electrolytes (Fig. 4b). When the depth of discharge increased to 50% with a capacity of 2.9 mAh cm‒2, a much longer durability of more than 145 h is achieved for Zn symmetric cells with NSQD additives (Fig. S8). Even at 20 mA cm−2 (1 mAh cm−2), the symmetric cell with NSQDs exhibits superior cycling stability compared to that with ZnSO4 or NQDs-containing electrolyte (Figs. 4c and S9). This long lifespan of nearly 6000 cycles (600 h) is attributed to the decreased concentration gradient with the negatively charged NSQD layer, which ensures an adequate Zn2+ supplement at a high rate. Moreover, the anode exhibits better cycling durability and cumulative capacity (CC) in comparison to the previously reported Zn anodes with carbon-based modification used in symmetric cells (Table S1).

In addition, SEM images show that the cycled Zn anode has smooth and compact Zn morphology with the NSQD additive, indicating uniform Zn plating/stripping during cycling (Fig. 4d). In contrast, numerous Zn dendrites deposited on the Zn surface can be observed (Fig. 4e). Moreover, the anode with the NSQD additive exhibits a stable CE for 600 cycles at 5 mA cm−2 (Figs. 4f and S10), and the average CE is as high as 99.8%. In contrast, the cell without the NSQD additive fails after 340 cycles, implying the unstable Zn plating/striping on Cu foil without the NSQD additive.

We further coupled Zn anodes with ZVO cathodes in full cells to investigate their electrochemical performance. The ZVO nanorods show the same morphologies and XRD patterns as reported (Fig. S11) [45]. The similar peaks in the CV curves prove the same redox process with or without the NSQD additive (Fig. S12). In comparison to the Zn//ZVO cell with normal electrolyte, the Zn//ZVO cell with NSQDs-containing electrolyte has higher specific capacities of 382.9 mAh g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 and 254.6 mAh g−1 at 4 A g−1 (Figs. 5 a and S13). As the current density increased to 10 A g−1, a high specific capacity of 208.4 mAh g−1 is retained, which is much better than that of Zn//ZVO cells with normal electrolytes (44.4 mAh g−1). In addition, The Zn//ZVO cell with NSQDs has a high specific capacity of 278.9 mAh g−1 when the current density was reset to 1 A g−1, indicating its excellent rate performance due to the mitigated Zn2+ concentration gradient (Fig. 5b).

The superior cycling stability arising from effective dendrite growth suppression ensures the long life of the Zn batteries. Fig. 5c shows the cycling stability of The Zn//ZVO cell with NSQDs and Zn//ZVO cells for 1500 cycles. The Zn//ZVO cell without NSQDs shows relatively poor capacity retention of 39.6%, whereas The Zn//ZVO cell with NSQDs shows good capacity retention of 85.9% with an average CE of 99.9%, which is excellent compared with previously reported Zn batteries with vanadium-based cathodes (Table S2).

We also conducted the EIS analysis and observed the morphological evolution of the Zn anode. The Zn//ZVO cell with NSQDs delivers a much smaller initial charge-transfer resistance (R ct) of 79 Ω in comparison to that of the Zn//ZVO cell (115 Ω), demonstrating the faster charge transfer and plating/striping kinetics of Zn at the Zn electrode (Fig. S14). In addition, the cycled Zn displays a smooth surface with uniform Zn deposition compared with the rough surface of Zn foil which has numerous dendrites (Fig. S15). Even with high mass loading (4.4 mg cm−2) of the cathode, the full cell with the NSQD additive also exhibits good cycling stability, achieving 91% capacity retention after 150 cycles (Fig. 5d). Thus, the improved cycling stability is mainly ascribed to reduced interfacial concentration gradient, which ensures the smooth Zn deposition morphology and suppressed side reactions during cycling.

To further the practical application, we used a pouch cell to test the cycling stability of the Zn anode with the NSQDs. The pouch full cell with the NSQD additive had a maximum capacity of 18 mAh and excellent cycling stability, retaining 81% capacity after 250 cycles (Fig. 5e). The good cycling stability is attributed to the dendrite-free Zn plating/stripping caused by the mitigated concentration gradient and homogenized electric field at the NSQD interface, which avoids short circuit during charge/discharge process.

In summary, we propose to uniformize the Zn nucleation and deposition by introducing the NSQD additive into the electrolyte, which mitigates the interfacial Zn2+ concentration gradient for uniform Zn deposition. With abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, the NSQDs react with the Zn foil, producing an effective NSQD layer. This formed NSQD layer offers abundant nucleation sites for the Zn ions pre-seeding. Meanwhile, the negatively charged carbon dots near the Zn surface uniformize the electric field and decrease the Zn2+ concentration gradient on the Zn-electrolyte interfaces, which is demonstrated by the finite simulation results. With a mitigated interfacial concentration gradient, the symmetric cell with the NSQD additive exhibits a good rate performance of 20 mA cm−2 for nearly 6000 cycles. The assembled full cell shows a high capacity retention of 85.9% after cycling for 1500 cycles. Even assembled in the pouch full cell, the full cell also exhibits excellent cycling stability with 81% of capacity retention after 250 cycles. This work demonstrates a simple method to stabilize the Zn anode and, more importantly, may provide a renewed understanding of interfacial concentration gradient regulation to suppress dendrite formation.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version.

Feifei Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Haotian Lu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Hanbing Zhu: Formal analysis. Lu Wang: Formal analysis. Zihui Chen: Formal analysis. Chunpeng Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Quan-Hong Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52202279), the National Industry-Education Integration Platform of Energy Storage, Haihe Laboratory of Sustainable Chemical Transformations, and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ensm.2023.03.032.
